top_items.title: Why Not Hyperparameter-Friendly Optimisation? A Monotonic Adaptive Norm Rescaling Approach For Long-Tailed Recognition
top_items.author_or_channel: Shuo Zhang, Chenqi Li, Tingting Zhu

definition: Normalization that standardizes each example across its own features rather than across the batch, making it entirely batch-independent. That single change removes batch normalization's weaknesses, no train/inference gap, no collapse at batch size one, clean handling of variable-length sequences, which is why it is the normalization of the transformer.

body.text: Layer normalization standardizes the activations within a single example, computing the mean and variance across that example's own feature dimensions rather than across a batch. It subtracts the mean, divides by the standard deviation, and applies a learned scale and shift, just as batch normalization does, but the statistics come from one example's features instead of from many examples' shared feature, which makes each example's normalization entirely independent of the others in the batch.

That batch independence is the whole point, because it removes batch normalization's central weaknesses. There is no difference between training and inference behavior, since no batch statistics are pooled; performance does not collapse when the batch is small or the batch size is one; and, decisively, it works cleanly on sequences of variable length. These are exactly the properties a sequence model needs, which is why layer normalization became the normalization of choice for the recurrent neural network and, above all, for the transformer.

The mechanism is simple and local: for a given token or hidden vector, layer normalization treats that vector's components as the population to normalize, so a token with unusually large activations is rescaled using only its own values. In a transformer a normalization layer wraps every attention mechanism sublayer and every feedforward sublayer, almost always with a residual connection, and modern designs favor the pre-norm arrangement, normalizing the input to each sublayer before the residual addition, because it keeps the residual path clean and makes training deep stacks more stable.

The choice between layer and batch normalization comes down to data shape and where the meaningful statistics live: batch normalization, normalizing across examples, suits the convolutional neural network on fixed-size image grids, while layer normalization, normalizing across features, suits sequence and attention models. Related variants like RMSNorm simplify the computation further by dropping the mean subtraction, and they are now common in large language models for their efficiency.

image.alt: Across the features of each example, an arbitrary distribution is rescaled to a normalized one; labeled features and layer normalization.
image.caption: Layer normalization rescales the activations within each individual example to a stable mean and variance, computed across its features rather than across the batch. That independence from batch size and sequence position is why it, not batch normalization, became the default inside transformers.
